title: Evolutionary drivers of the hump-shaped latitudinal gradient of benthic polychaete species richness along the Southeastern Pacific coast
evidence: The paper states "four were significant (median latitudinal range, SST, salinity and SST range; ranked according to its importance, see Table 2)." The key conclusion is: "the prevalence of the median latitudinal range as the most important predictor of the species richness, being also the predictor with a high degree of phylogenetic conservatism, strongly supports the idea of an extra-tropical origin for many polychaetes species." Variable importance uses "conditional random forest which accounts for the possible predictor collinearity (Strobl et al., 2008), based on the method proposed by Altmann et al. (2010), with p-values based on 10,000 permutations." Table 2 shows importance rankings (Median lat. range: 831.525, SST: 732.814, Salinity: 541.348, SST range: 217.978) for variables with different scales and collinearity (VIF 3.18-9.33).

title: Killer whale acoustic patterns respond to prey abundance and environmental variability around the Prince Edward Islands, Southern Ocean
evidence: The paper states: "Random forest modelling identified wind speed as the primary predictor of the occurrence of killer whale calls (with a negative correlation) while sea surface height, chlorophyll-a and sea surface temperature were moderately important" and "Wind speed was the most important predictor of the occurrence of echolocation clicks and social calls." The discussion interprets these rankings ecologically: "Wind speed was the most important predictor... as their occurrence in October to December coincided with low wind speed." No alternative importance measures (permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance) or held-out predictive validation support the magnitude and ranking.

title: Machine learning model of the catalytic efficiency and substrate specificity of acyl-ACP thioesterase variants generated from natural and in vitro directed evolution
evidence: The paper states: "Feature importance scores for each residue position... were calculated based on the total decrease in node gini-impurity." Residues were "ranked in descending order based on their importance scores" and the final model contained "the top 22 residue positions." The central finding is that "the majority of highest scoring residues (17 out of 22 residues) reside within the N-terminal hot-dog structure," directly depending on which specific residues ranked highest. Cross-validation assessed model classification performance (recall 92%, MCC 0.87) but not importance magnitude validation. Only 6 of 22 residues had prior experimental validation; the remaining 16 were identified new by the importance ranking.

title: The role of external factors on the reactivation of the heritage language of Turkish-German returnees
evidence: The paper presents random-forest importance rankings in Tables 4-5 and interprets which predictors are "most important" and "critical factors": "frequency of Turkish use emerged as a critical factor" and "The most important predictor, in this case, is the time a returnee spent in Germany." These magnitude/ranking claims directly answer the study's main research question ("What external factors modulate the attainment in the (re-)activated HL?") but lack independent validation via permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation. Partial dependence plots visualize the fitted model but do not corroborate the importance ranking.

title: Monthly and Diel Acoustic Occurrence of Four Baleen Whale Species in South African Waters
evidence: The paper concludes: "Sea surface height and sea surface temperature, either separately or in combination, were the most important predictors of whale acoustic occurrence, highlighting the influence of environmental conditions on the distribution, habitat selection, and ecology of these whales" (abstract). This ranking-based conclusion is unsupported by independent corroboration methods. While Figure 6a shows partial dependence influence curves for the relationships, the paper relies on importance ranking from ranger's default Gini importance (scaled to percentages) to claim which predictors are "most important" versus "moderately important" versus "least important" (Results, Section 3.5). Statistical significance testing uses Altmann's permutation method, which only tests whether importance != 0, not whether the ranking is reliable or resistant to bias from skewed features (chlorophyll-a) or high-cardinality categorical features (hour with 24 levels).

title: Leveraging Scheme for Cross-Study Microbiome Machine Learning Prediction and Feature Evaluations
evidence: "The top features were defined as the highest average normalized hits across 100 iterations" and the random forest importance (Z-scores) are evaluated and compared with shuffled replicas to determine importance. The paper makes biomarker selections based on importance ranking: "these consistently high-importance taxa are good candidates for disease-related microbial biomarkers." While ANCOM-BC differential abundance provides some validation for CRC and CD, this corroboration is incomplete and fails entirely for immunotherapy ("ANCOM-BC did not identify any genus and species-level taxa as being differentially abundant"), yet the importance-based biomarker claims remain central to the conclusions without reliance on permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation.

title: Siderophores as an iron source for picocyanobacteria in deep chlorophyll maximum layers of the oligotrophic ocean
evidence: The paper states it uses "the relative importance obtained from the feature importance algorithm" (Ranger default = impurity importance) to weight PCs and rank variables. It explicitly claims: "Of all trace metals in the global dataset, dFe measured from GEOTRACES samples explained most of the weighted variance across the principal component predictors used in the random forest." Based on importance rankings, the authors conclude that "dFe's predictive power in the random forest was primarily derived from its nonlinear associations and interactions with other environmental variables" despite showing dFe has weak linear correlation (r = -0.10). These importance-based interpretations are central to supporting the paper's main conclusion that specific environmental variables drive siderophore consumer biogeography.

title: Prediction of long-term survival after gastrectomy using random survival forests
evidence: The paper ranks variables as "the most important variables were number of positive lymph nodes, pT stage and completeness of resection" based on impurity importance filtering via Boruta ("comparing the importance of candidate variables in a random forest to a corresponding set of 'shadow' variables"). This ranking is central to conclusions: "The most important variables identified (lymph node status, pT stage, resection margin) are well recognised as highly prognostic." However, no independent corroboration is provided—survival curves and RMST/LED/LER metrics derive from the same impurity-based model. Lymph node counts are count data likely heavily skewed, vulnerable to impurity-importance magnification.

title: Oropharyngeal microbiome profiled at admission is predictive of the need for respiratory support among COVID-19 patients
evidence: The paper ranks microbiome features by importance and selects which to highlight based on rank position. Key passages: (1) "Based on the median calculated permutated variable importance, a ranking of which features contributed most to the prediction...the relative abundance of P. salivae is the most important predictor" and "ranked higher than both patient age and BMI"; (2) "Decreased P. salivae abundance was the strongest and most general predictor"; (3) Conclusion claims species with high importance "contribute to COVID-19 respiratory symptoms" and "the decreased abundance of some organisms, primarily P. salivae, is predictive." No corroboration via permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the ranking itself.

title: Combining Environmental Variables and Machine Learning Methods to Determine the Most Significant Factors Influencing Honey Production
evidence: The paper uses Random Forest impurity importance (via Ranger package) to rank features and states: "Among the 18 features retained after the feature selection process, the average temperature and pressure in February (T2M_2 and PS_2), as well as the average wind speed in January (WS2M_1), emerged as the most significant predictors" (Sect. 3.4). Figure 2 displays "Feature importance based on Random Forest" with explicit ranking. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or held-out validation of importance is provided. Boruta is used for feature selection (zero/non-zero detection) but not to validate the ranking. Discussion and conclusions hinge on this ranking: "Beekeepers can use pre-harvest weather parameters, such as winter temperature, humidity, atmospheric pressure, and wind speed, to inform management strategies" (Conclusions). Dataset contains multiple correlated monthly variants (T2M_12, T2M_1, T2M_2, T2M_MAX_1/12/2, T2M_MIN_1/12/2; RH2M variants; WS2M variants in different months), EVI at 5 different months, and features spanning disparate scales (pressure ~99 kPa, temperature ~2-15°C, wind ~1 m/s, EVI ~0.4-0.55).

title: Novel Associations Between Mid-Pregnancy Cardiovascular Biomarkers and Preeclampsia: An Explorative Nested Case-Control Study
evidence: The paper uses Boruta, which "is built on random survival forest (RF), which determines an unbiased grading of the predictive importance." Results emphasize ranking: "in descending order of importance: PlGF, MMP-12, LOX-1..." and "MMP-12 is highlighted as a promising predictive mid-pregnancy biomarker" and "BNP and IDUA were the two strongest biomarkers." The conclusion states the study "found MMP-12 to be the most promising candidate." Logistic regression was applied only to examine "the direction of the individual association" and found significant associations only for late-onset, not validating the Boruta rankings through alternative methods.

title: Predictive features of gene expression variation reveal mechanistic link with differential expression
evidence: The paper states: 'We retrieved the features' "importance score" from the Boruta algorithm' using 'Z-scores of mean decrease accuracy measure as importance attribute.' The paper then ranks features as 'the strongest predictor of variation' and constructs mechanistic conclusions: 'Two classes of genes with low variation have distinct regulatory mechanisms' based on which features rank highest (broad promoter TF occupancy vs. narrow promoter Pol II pausing). These ranking claims lack alternative validation: Boruta tests only that features 'predict better than their permuted version' (binary selection), not magnitude. Cross-validation provides held-out performance but not corroboration of feature ranking robustness to impurity bias.

title: Post-fire structural forest recovery associated with climate extremes in dry sub-boreal forests
evidence: Paper states "In the global model, anomalously cold and dry summers were the most important predictors of observed groupings...Fire impacts...and site-level/environmental conditions...had similar relative importance values (41-45%). Topographic position had a notably lower importance...We found pre-fire basal area (BA) was the third most important driver." Main conclusion: "post-fire climate anomalies best predicted structural recovery." These ranking and magnitude interpretations are unsupported by permutation importance, SHAP, or held-out validation; Boruta only validates that variables are used (importance > noise), not their ranking.

title: Machine Learning Predicts the Presence of 2,4,6-Trinitrotoluene in Sediments of a Baltic Sea Munitions Dumpsite Using Microbial Community Compositions
evidence: The paper states: "Importance for community data (0.08% threshold, genus rank) and combined data was calculated utilizing the corrected Gini impurity (Nembrini et al., 2018), followed by p-value estimation after Janitza et al. (2018)." The central claim identifies "potential microbial fingerprints" by ranking: "Thus, 25 genera were selected with Janitza importance > 0.25, p < 0.01" and "The most supportive genera (Figure 5) were Cocleimonas (1.65% maximal relative abundance), the unclassified Anaerolineae SBR1031 A4b (0.11%) and an unclassified Gemmatimonadaceae (0.38%)." Notably, the authors explicitly used permutation importance (Altmann) for sediment data but not for the 25 bacterial genera that form their fingerprint. The data are compositional (1,703 genera available, highly skewed abundances), creating conditions for impurity-bias to distort both magnitude and ranking.

title: Disentangling soybean GxE effects in an integrated genomic prediction and machine learning-GWAS workflow
evidence: The paper states: "For creating a 'small' Random Forest model, the uncorrelated markers with high VI were incrementally added as model features, starting with the markers with the highest VI." Results show "22 markers were detected for R8, 15 for Y and 9 for P" at f>3 thresholds. The authors acknowledge that "the VI can be biased" but rely on Janitza et al. p-value adjustment via f-factor. However, they never corroborate the importance ranking with alternative methods (no SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance). While held-out validation confirms the selected 50 markers achieve "similar predictive ability," this does not validate the ranking of importance among those markers, which the impurity bias could have distorted.

title: Drug product attributes predict clinical efficacy in betibeglogene autotemcel gene therapy for β-thalassemia
evidence: The paper concludes: "The top predictive features were found to be drug product quality attributes... Figure 2 A shows that the %LVV+ cells are the most predictive attribute of PB VCN at month 6 and subsequently TI" and uses this ranking to establish clinical "acceptance criteria for future release and clinical use of beti-cel." This critical ranking relies solely on ranger impurity importance; no permutation importance on validation set, SHAP values, PDP/ALE plots, or external cohort validation corroborate the ranking. The %LVV+ variable is a percentage measure with probable rightward skew, precisely where impurity importance magnifies effects, and multiple correlated transduction efficiency surrogates exist that could have had importance mis-attributed.

title: Knockout of immunotherapy prognostic marker genes eliminates the effect of the anti-PD-1 treatment
evidence: The paper explicitly uses "random survival forest (RSF) approach" to rank pathways and genes by "feature importance (RSF variable importance, p-value < 0.05)." Based on this ranking, they select pathways "with the greatest bearing on predictive power" and construct a final biomarker panel from the "top 40 IPMGs," stating that "the model taking the expression of the top 40 IPMGs as input achieved the highest 88.91% accuracy." Their main conclusion is that "the expression of the 40 IPMGs can serve as a set of pan-cancer prognostic biomarkers for immunotherapies." This relies directly on the ranking being correct to identify which genes should be included in the panel.

title: How Have Global River Widths Changed Over Time?
evidence: "The most important factor driving temporal variations in river width is the climate for natural rivers and soil condition for regulated rivers" (stated 17 times throughout). The paper calculates importance as "node impurity and calculated as the decrease in the residual sum of squares" and directly interprets magnitude (e.g., "Elevation is the most important parameter influencing river width variability"). No permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation provided. ALE plots used only for visualization, not to validate rankings. Paper acknowledges results are exploratory and "further investigations are needed to explain why/how climate and soil erosion impact river width."

title: Machine learning-based chemical binding similarity using evolutionary relationships of target genes
evidence: The authors quantify feature contribution using "variable importance (Gini index)" to rank evolutionary information types as Family, Target, and PFAM (high importance) versus TS-SMART, TS-TIGR, TS-PRINT, and TS-Gene3D (low importance). They directly interpret this ranking to explain model performance differences ("probably due to the low model performance and target coverage") without permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance validation. Features are heterogeneous in type (sequence-based domains vs structure-based superfamilies) and coverage (PFAM/Family/Superfamily annotate >74% of targets; SMART/TIGRFAM/PRINT/Gene3D <50%), conditions under which Gini importance is biased upward for high-coverage features regardless of true predictive contribution. While held-out AUC test sets validate overall model performance, they do not validate the importance ranking itself.

title: The pan-tropical age distribution of regenerating tropical moist forest
evidence: The paper uses "corrected impurity measure which estimates mean decrease in accuracy" via ranger to rank importance of 32 predictors. The core conclusions depend on this ranking: "Location variables, including longitude, latitude and country had high predictive power...Forest landscape characteristics tended to be very important...Factors relating directly to forms of human pressure...had little influence." These importance-based rankings drive policy recommendations that "restoration efforts...should target areas with high surrounding forest landscape integrity." The feature space is vulnerable to impurity bias: data are "heavily skewed towards younger forests" (52% age ≤5 years), include mixed variable types (continuous environmental, high-cardinality categorical locations like country, landscape indices), and lack corroboration beyond partial dependence plots (which show shape, not relative importance).

title: Extending Classification Algorithms to Case-Control Studies
evidence: Table 1 specifies RF variable importance as "Gini index." The paper states: "To determine which biomolecules were most predictive of the case/control status of each individual, the features were ranked within each source using a variable importance metric appropriate for each classification algorithm." Table 5 presents ranked importances and the Discussion concludes: "In terms of the variables selected by the different methods (Table 5), CLR and CSVM-RBF selected the same 2 metabolites as the 2 most important features: alpha-tocopherol and apidic acid...Both methods found these 2 metabolites to be highly important." The paper employs no permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional, or heldout validation of the Gini-based ranking. Feature sets include metabolites, lipids, and SNPs with scales ranging -0.079 to 23.149 (metabolites) vs 0 to 2.0 (SNPs).

title: Machine learning prediction of refractory ventricular fibrillation in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest using features available to EMS
evidence: The paper states: "From feature importance analysis, it was found that the top 3 features for the RF model were age, public arrest location, and heart disease... In comparison, the top 3 features for the multivariable logistic regression and LASSO model were cancer, public arrest location, and male gender." The Discussion explicitly interprets rankings: "Based on the model features, male and public location were the variables which highly contributed to the model. We believe it may be reasonable due to the following reasons. Generally, males were reported to be the predominant population... Furthermore, OHCA cases with VF were reported to occur more commonly in public locations..." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance corroboration is provided for these interpretations, despite an imbalanced outcome (4.1% positive) and mixed feature types that amplify impurity importance bias.

title: Do little interactions get lost in dark random forests?
evidence: The paper's core claims rest entirely on interpreting the magnitude and ranking of Gini importance: "Of the single importance measures, the Gini importance ranked the interacting SNPs generally higher than the permutation importance" and "The Gini importance performs better than the permutation importance in identifying SNPs involved in an interaction." Central to the conclusions is the claim that "in most of the cases, the interactions are masked by marginal effects and interactions cannot be differentiated from marginal effects"—a ranking-based finding that depends on comparing importance scores of interacting SNPs versus marginal-only SNPs. The paper shows comparative results against permutation importance (which ranks variables differently), but this is comparison rather than corroboration; no independent validation via SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation is provided to confirm that the Gini importance ranking truly reflects underlying variable roles.

title: Perinatal mortality in German dairy cattle: Unveiling the importance of cow-level risk factors and their interactions using a multifaceted modelling approach
evidence: The paper states: "The Chi-Square statistics...and mean decrease accuracy...both ranked the importance of the five predictors identically, showing calving ease and parity to be the most influential predictors for PM, with calving ease showing a much higher importance for PM compared to parity. The brute-force model selection approach ranked parity as the most influential predictor, closely followed by calving ease and breed." However, these three methods show discrepant rankings (brute-force differs on whether parity or calving ease is #1), and no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or held-out validation is used. The abstract centrally claims: "The difficulty of calving, breed and parity of the cow were found to be the most important factors, followed by farm size and season," driving breed-specific recommendations without robust corroboration of the importance magnitudes or rankings. The balanced random forest's sensitivity to class imbalance (unbalanced RF ranked breed second vs. third) is acknowledged but not addressed through robust methods."

title: Determination of Survival of Gastric Cancer Patients With Distant Lymph Node Metastasis Using Prealbumin Level and Prothrombin Time: Contour Plots Based on Random Survival Forest Algorithm on High-Dimensionality Clinical and Laboratory Datasets
evidence: The paper's central claim is that 'the prealbumin level and the PT were found to be the most crucial factors' based on feature selection where 'the prealbumin level and the PT were identified as the predictors of top priority. Their importance was much greater than those of the other factors.' This ranking-based conclusion depends entirely on impurity importance. While partial dependence plots are provided for the selected variables to show effect shapes, no cross-variable comparison is performed to validate the ranking across all 81 features, and the authors note that 'the distributions of the PT...are left-skewed'—precisely the scenario where impurity importance magnification bias peaks. No permutation importance, SHAP, or conditional importance analysis is provided to verify the ranking is robust to the known biases of Gini/MDI-based importance.

title: Biological knowledge-slanted random forest approach for the classification of calcified aortic valve stenosis
evidence: The paper uses selection frequency across trees to rank genes and identifies ATP6V0D2 as the "main gene used to classify the cases" and reports "the 20 top genes." The substantive claim is that "knowledge-slanted RF could be more easily interpreted by users because the prediction can be attributed mainly to associated genes." However, ranking is validated only via post-hoc univariate ANOVA on differential expression (conditional on prior selection), not via permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or held-out validation of classification importance.

title: Predicting Clinical Remission of Chronic Urticaria Using Random Survival Forests: Machine Learning Applied to Real-World Data
evidence: The paper uses ranger random survival forests and identifies features as "among the 20 most important predictors" and "among the 50 most important predictors," ranking them by impurity-based importance. Key claim: "Clinically relevant features identified by the model to have the most predictive value for the time to clinical remission included age at index, time from first recorded CU-related symptom to first CU diagnosis, BMI, smoking status, asthma, allergic rhinitis, chronic pulmonary disease, and prior medication use." Only one variable (age) receives partial dependence validation shown in supplementary materials; other top-ranked categorical and binary comorbidity variables lack corroboration. Discussion emphasizes these identified predictors as central to understanding disease pathogenesis: "The potential for the characteristics identified as important by the predictive model, such as comorbidities, polypharmacy, and increased laboratory workup, to help predict time to clinical remission raise intriguing questions regarding the pathogenesis of CU."

title: A first-generation pediatric cancer dependency map
evidence: The paper identifies "the most important feature in predicting MCL1 dependency was BCL2L1 expression" and "the top predictive feature for MDM2 dependency was RNA expression of EDA2R, a known direct target of p53" and "Predictive feature modeling for HDAC2 highlighted RNA expression of its known target FUCA1." These ranking-based feature importance statements are used to make biological inferences, but the Methods section provides no description of how importance was calculated (scikit-learn default is Gini/MDI). No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or other robust validation of the feature importance rankings is reported; bivariate correlations shown (Fig 8b) do not validate the multivariate feature importance ranking.

title: Analysis of the Frictional Performance of AW-5251 Aluminium Alloy Sheets Using the Random Forest Machine Learning Algorithm and Multilayer Perceptron
evidence: The paper ranks variables by "mean decrease in accuracy," concluding oil viscosity (0.59) is far more important than Ra (0.30) or K/Rp0.2 (0.05-0.06). These magnitude and ranking claims are central to conclusions like "K and the other inputs...was the least important one." ALE plots and ANNs provide partial corroboration that viscosity and Ra drive friction, but permutation importance, SHAP, or held-out predictive validation are absent. Feature scales range over orders of magnitude (Ra: 0.3-1.25 µm; viscosity: 14-44 mm²/s; Rp0.2: 68-212 MPa; K: 242-370 MPa), creating conditions where impurity importance can magnify apparent effect size differences.

title: Predicting Response to Neuromodulators or Prokinetics in Patients With Suspected Gastroparesis Using Machine Learning: The “BMI, Infectious Prodrome, Delayed GES, and No Diabetes” Model
evidence: The paper uses the R package vip to extract variable importance from a ranger random forest model without specifying the importance type, defaulting to impurity-based (Gini) importance. The authors explicitly rank variables by importance magnitude: "Delayed GES was the most important predictor by variable importance analysis, followed by absence of diabetes, BMI, and infectious prodrome" and state clinical conclusions directly tied to these rankings such as "increase in BMI was predictive of response" while "delayed GES, absence of diabetes, and infectious prodrome were predictors of nonresponse." They validate on a held-out test set but provide no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance corroboration of the variable rankings.

title: Applying traditional and machine learning-based GWAS approaches for marker-trait identification in wheat
evidence: The paper explicitly uses "decrease of node impurity" for Random Forest and "gain" for XGBoost. Methods section states: "As feature importance metrics, we selected gain for XGB and decrease of node impurity for RF instead of permutation-based importance." The results interpret magnitude and ranking: "RF model identified 44 highly important markers, with major loci on chromosomes 6A and 6D" and "XGB algorithm highlighted highly important loci." While MTAs are cross-validated across traditional and ML methods, this validation addresses which variables are selected (yes/no), not whether the magnitude and ranking of impurity importance is correct. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance validation is provided.

title: Use of QSAR Global Models and Molecular Docking for Developing New Inhibitors of c-src Tyrosine Kinase
evidence: The paper uses RF-based impurity importance from three separate algorithms (randomForest, randomForestSRC, ranger packages) for feature selection. The Discussion prominently ranks features by importance: "By far the most important descriptor in our work, identified by multiple feature selection algorithms, was SpMax4_Bh(m)" and "The second most important descriptor for our data set was DECC." The Results section emphasizes that "the first five [descriptors] explained the largest part of the variability in the training and testing datasets." The features are heterogeneous across 19 descriptor types (1596 features for 2D atom pairs alone) with mixed numerical scales. While the paper used permutation importance as one of 17 selection algorithms and external model validation (docking, PubChem/ChEMBL), it does not validate the RANKING or MAGNITUDE of feature importance via permutation-based or SHAP methods to confirm the RF-based importance rankings hold robustly.

title: Spring phenology drives range shifts in a migratory Arctic ungulate with key implications for the future
evidence: The paper explicitly uses "improvement in Gini impurity" for variable selection and importance calculation. Figure 2 ranks phenology variables by importance, claiming "The most important variable was the proportion of low shrub cover at the 10-km scale followed by snow melt and onset of greenness dates." The core finding that caribou are driven by specific phenological windows relies on these importance rankings without permutation or SHAP corroboration. The paper makes no mention of p-values for importance statistics and uses only partial dependence plots as supplementary visualization of variable relationships.

title: Interpretable multi-omics machine learning reveals drought-driven shifts in plant-microbe interactions
evidence: The paper states "RF constructs a collection of decision trees and aggregates their predictions, providing both high predictive performance and feature importance scores for biological features" and explicitly interprets feature ranking: "Under drought stress, the most highly contributing features were predominantly flavonoids, including isoflavones (e.g. daidzin and its derivatives and genistin)." The abstract concludes that "SHAP indicated that the isoflavone derivative, daidzin, and the drought-tolerant Candidatus Nitrosocosmicus, were major contributors to phenotypic variation." The paper uses SHAP for post-hoc interpretation of an RF importance-ranked model, not permutation importance or other magnitude-corroborating methods prior to drawing conclusions about feature ranking and magnitude.

title: Not just form, not just meaning: Words with consistent form-meaning mappings are learned earlier
evidence: The paper uses Random Forest regression via ranger package and states "the importance of a variable to the regression task can be easily computed and provides insights about its role in predicting the target variable," then interprets magnitude and ranking: "PSC ld, in particular, is slightly more important than concreteness and more important than most other predictors" and "PSC ld is more important than iconicity in predicting AoA and ranks just below concreteness." Features include binary morphological complexity, continuous measures, and count-based predictors (PND, frequency) that are typically right-skewed. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out test validation is used; corroboration relies on linear regression (validating significance/direction, not importance magnitude) and GAMs (validating non-linearity check), neither of which address impurity importance bias to magnitude and ranking.

title: Random forest-based bioavailable strontium isoscape for environmental and archaeological applications in central eastern Argentina and western Uruguay
evidence: The paper reports both Increase in Mean Squared Error (%IncMSE, permutation-based) and Increase in Node Purity (IncNodePurity, impurity-based) importance measures. The authors interpret magnitude and ranking to conclude: "r.elevation emerged as the most predictive in %IncMSE, with high values in %IncMSE (~20%) and IncNodePurity (~0.9 × 10-4), highlighting its relevance in predicting the 87 Sr/ 86 Sr ratio." They then use this ranking to explain geological controls: "For this reason, elevation becomes a predictive variable because it indirectly differentiates the type of sediments: fluvial and marine deposits of the Paraná Delta and the elevated plains with loessic/continental deposits." The disagreement between %IncMSE and IncNodePurity for r.dust (~15% permutation vs ~0.2 × 10-4 impurity) is not investigated and suggests potential non-linear effects or interactions where impurity bias operates. While permutation importance provides partial corroboration, the paper lacks SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance validation to confirm that importance rankings reflect actual variable effects rather than bias artifacts, especially given the presence of mixed-type features with scale mismatches and skewed distributions.

title: Fully automated detection of paramagnetic rims in multiple sclerosis lesions on 3T susceptibility-based MR imaging
evidence: The paper explicitly computes variable importance as "the percent increase in mean-squared error for a model with the variable over a model with a permuted version of that variable, scaled for comparability across variables" (Methods 2.7) and reports: "The random forest identified uniformity, entropy, and energy as the most important radiomic features for classifying lesions." It then interprets rankings: "The radiomic features that were the most important in this context aimed to measure the variability of intensity within a lesion (entropy and uniformity) or quantify the magnitudes of the intensities themselves (energy)" (Discussion). These conclusions about which features "are most important" and why they matter biologically are drawn from impurity importance without corroboration via permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation.

title: Global mapping of potential natural vegetation: an assessment of machine learning algorithms for estimating land potential
evidence: The conclusions state findings explicitly based on importance ranking: "the most important predictors being total annual precipitation, monthly temperatures, and bioclimatic layers" and "Precipitation, temperature maps, and bioclimatic images are consistently the most important covariates in all three case studies." The paper also ranks predictors ("top 20 most important") and interprets magnitude ("decline in variable importance was gradual"). Corroboration is limited to cross-validation accuracy and ecological plausibility arguments; the paper provides no permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional, or held-out validation of importance rankings.

title: SARS-CoV-2 detection status associates with bacterial community composition in patients and the hospital environment
evidence: "The paper states: 'the importance of each ASV for the prediction performance of the four classifiers...was estimated by the built-in random forest scores in the 100-fold cross-validation. For each body site or environmental site, we finally ranked all ASVs by their average ranking of importance scores.' The main contribution is identifying 'the top 100 ranked important ASV's' and reporting 'One ASV with an exact match to Rothia dentocariosa...was highly ranked as predictive across all four disparate sample types' and 'ASVs from the genera Actinomyces, Anaerococcus, Dialister, Gemella, and Schaalia were in the top 40 ranked predictive features.' These rankings drive their biological conclusions about which taxa are most associated with SARS-CoV-2."

title: A comparative study of forest methods for time-to-event data: variable selection and predictive performance
evidence: The paper evaluates variable selection performance by "frequency of ranking top of the variable importance measures of the correct variables" and concludes "MSR-RF and CIF have higher selection rates" than RSF, with "MSR-RF possess practical value and is well worth popularizing." However, the authors themselves observe that "higher correct variable selection frequency does not match better IBS or c-index value exactly in simulation study," indicating VIM-based ranking does not correlate with predictive validity. No permutation importance, SHAP, or conditional importance corroboration is provided, leaving method-ranking conclusions vulnerable to impurity-importance bias, especially for RSF which the paper acknowledges exhibits "selection bias towards covariates with more possible split points."

title: Predicting drug activity against cancer cells by random forest models based on minimal genomic information and chemical properties
evidence: The paper identifies top oncogenes by ranking Gini impurity indices: "The four oncogenes whose mutational statuses are the most important variables...are TP53, BRAF, MYC, and CREBBP, which have Gini impurity indices of 108, 105, 96, and 86" and selects features based on this ranking without permutation or SHAP validation of importance ranking. Although model performance is validated through cross-validation and the identified genes show biological plausibility, the magnitude and ranking of impurity importance are not corroborated by alternative importance methods.

title: Human Activity Changes During COVID‐19 Lockdown in China—A View From Nighttime Light
evidence: The paper uses feature contributions from random forest models and explicitly interprets magnitude and ranking: "For each of the six categories, the contribution of the sample to the target value was calculated, and then medians of feature contributions as a proxy for the 'standard level' of variable contributions were obtained with higher absolute values indicating a larger contribution." The entire Discussion (4.1) depends on interpreting these magnitudes to explain city-specific patterns: "For Beijing (Figure 6a), the contribution of the company gradually declined from stage I to IV and recovered in stage V." No permutation importance, SHAP, or conditional importance validation exists; OOB validation only checks overall fit (R² ~0.52), not feature-ranking robustness. Conclusions state "companies in all cities were resuscitated during stationary period, but Guangzhou's recovery was earlier" - a ranking claim unsupported by alternative importance methods.

title: The current status and shortcomings of stereotactic radiosurgery
evidence: "Random forest modeling identified the size of the field as the most important factor for passing or failing followed by algorithm" and "collimator was found to be among the least important parameters in predicting phantom performance." The paper concludes "Field size had the largest impact in determining performance" and recommends treatment parameters needing "particular attention...include smaller field sizes and pencil beam algorithms," making the importance ranking the basis for clinical guidance. The authors note field size and collimator correlate (cone collimators used for majority of small fields), yet attribute the effect to field size as "the driving force," which is vulnerable to impurity-importance bias that favors continuous over categorical variables.

title: Machine Learning Applied to Omics Datasets Predicts Mortality in Patients with Alcoholic Hepatitis
evidence: The paper uses random forest feature importance to "select variables with the top 11 average feature importance in each multi-omics data" (Section 2.2) and interprets biological meaning from rankings: "Among the selected bacteria and metabolic pathways used for the 30-day mortality prediction, 6 pathways were related to purine nucleoside biosynthesis. Nucleotide biosynthesis has been reported to be critical for the growth of bacteria in human blood." No corroboration via permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance is provided, and the heterogeneous omics data (529 bacterial species, 472 pathways, 287 lipids, plus metabolites and clinical labs with different scales and distributions) amplifies impurity-importance bias risk.

title: Soil carbon in the world’s tidal marshes
evidence: The paper explicitly states: "Within the final random forest, variable importance was set to 'impurity' within the ranger package, corresponding to the Gini index for classification." It interprets both ranking and magnitude: "Soil depth was the most important driver of SOC density" and "temperature (both maximum and minimum) had similar relative variable importance as many other covariates." The authors acknowledge a key limitation: "While the variable importance of the model indicates that temperatures partially drive the patterns of SOC, there is uncertainty in this assessment as underlying interactions between variables are not captured." No permutation importance, SHAP, partial dependence, conditional importance, or held-out validation is used to corroborate these rankings.

title: VariantSpark: Cloud-based machine learning for association study of complex phenotype and large-scale genomic data
evidence: The paper ranks genomic variants by Gini-impurity importance scores and claims VariantSpark "outperforms LR for epistatic and complex phenotypes," achieving detection rates "up to 4.6 times higher than LR." These substantive conclusions rest entirely on importance ranking (top-k variant recovery), with no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation to corroborate the ranking. The ranking-based findings are central to the paper's core message about VariantSpark's superiority.

title: Global analysis and prediction of fluoride in groundwater
evidence: The Methods section states importance was "determined as the mean decrease of the Gini index (classification impurity)" and the paper concludes "The geospatial prediction model is dominated by climate parameters" based explicitly on Gini rankings in Fig. 2a. While univariate Kendall correlations in Fig. 2b show relationships between individual predictors and fluoride prevalence, no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance methods are employed to validate the importance magnitude or ranking. The authors acknowledge the bias directly: "a categorical variable (such as lithology) is less likely than a continuous variable to have an effect on the model when its values are randomly sorted, due to having far fewer possible values," yet implement no correction. The predictor set mixes continuous (climate, topography, soil at 250m-1km resolution) and categorical (acidic igneous rocks) variables with inherently different cardinalities, creating conditions for impurity importance to magnify effects on high-cardinality continuous features.

title: kernInt: A Kernel Framework for Integrating Supervised and Unsupervised Analyses in Spatio-Temporal Metagenomic Datasets
evidence: Paper explicitly uses mean decrease in node impurity and mean decrease Gini for RF feature importance. Authors interpret biological drivers from feature ranking: soil data show "prediction is primarily driven by few OTUs of extreme pH ecosystems (e.g., genera Rubrobacter and Balneimonas)"; pig data note "great importance given by cLin to Desulfovibrio." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out predictive validation of feature ranking is provided to corroborate these importance magnitude and ranking claims.

title: Using machine learning to guide targeted and locally-tailored empiric antibiotic prescribing in a children's hospital in Cambodia
evidence: The abstract states: "Most important variables for predicting susceptibility were time from admission to blood culture, patient age, hospital versus community-acquired infection, and age-adjusted weight score." Figure 5 reports that "the most important predictor for resistance to ceftriaxone is patient age (leaving this out would decrease the model accuracy 100% of the time). Patient age is closely followed by days from hospital admission to blood sample, age-adjusted weight score, and the classification of the infection as hospital- or community-acquired (omitting this variable would decrease model accuracy 75% of the time)." The paper uses ranger package random forests (default impurity-based importance), cites Janitza et al. for methodology, but reports no p-values or significance tests for importance scores. No corroboration via permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation is provided. The "days from admission" variable has exponential distribution (Table 1), making it susceptible to upward-biased impurity importance.

title: Haemosporidian Infection Risk Variation Across an Urban Gradient in a Songbird
evidence: The paper identifies significant predictors using Gini importance thresholds: "The predictor variables were considered significant only if they exceeded the absolute values of the variable with the lowest Gini impurity." Three variables are ranked by importance magnitude (wing chord 0.494 > trash 0.38 > tables 0.375) and interpreted as "the most significant variables for predicting infection risk." The core conclusion states: "biotic factors—rather than abiotic environmental conditions—may play a more significant role in increasing infection risk." No independent validation method (permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance) corroborates these importance rankings.

title: Discovering single nucleotide variants and indels from bulk and single-cell ATAC-seq
evidence: Paper explicitly reports feature importance rankings based on Gini impurity decrease: "the most important feature was GATK's QD, followed by GQ" and "GATK had the highest summed feature importance, followed by VarDict and VarScan2." Uses these rankings to guide substantive methodological decisions—selecting which callers to include: "Some of the variant callers did not provide features that contributed substantially to the classification accuracy, as indicated by the summed feature importances." No corroboration via permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance methods. Validation is limited to model performance (precision/recall) on held-out test data, which does not validate the feature importance rankings themselves.

title: Using Machine Learning to Evaluate the Value of Genetic Liabilities in the Classification of Hypertension within the UK Biobank
evidence: Methods state: "The most important features were ranked based on their importance score and illustrated using variable importance plot (vip) function within the caret package." Results show ranked importance: "age as the most important classifying feature for hypertension, followed by sex, BMI, TC, LDL, sedentary lifestyle, HDL, TC genetic liability, LDL genetic liability, and smoking status." Conclusions rest on this ranking: "genetic liability for two lipid traits (TC and LDL) was found to improve the classification of hypertension." Discussion draws substantive biological inferences from the ranking: "The observation that our machine learning feature selection approach did not prioritize diabetes as an important feature in classifying hypertension may align with the existing inquiries in the literature." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance methods are used to corroborate the ranking.

title: Resting state connectivity best predicts alcohol use severity in moderate to heavy alcohol users
evidence: Feature importance was evaluated using an impurity measure based on standardized response variance in prediction of AUDIT across trees, with rankings presented in Table 2. The core conclusion that Network connectivity features between salience network, default mode network, executive control network, and sensory networks explained 33% of the variance depends entirely on these impurity-based importance scores and rankings. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance analysis validates the feature ranking, although the model generalizes to held-out validation data.

title: DNA Methylation in INA, NHLH2, and THBS4 Is Associated with Metastatic Disease in Renal Cell Carcinoma
evidence: The paper states: "Random forest classification analysis was carried out following optimization of the random forest model parameters using the R libraries caret and ranger. The importance of variables was calculated and presented using the randomForestExplainer package," employing "mean decrease in gini" metrics. The results explicitly interpret the importance ranking: "Importance analysis of the variables suggested that 6 of the 7 (86%) INA, 2 of the 4 (50%) NHLH2, and 2 of the 7 (29%) THBS4 annotated CpG loci were the most important predictors." The paper uses this ranking to identify "a subset of 10 candidate loci from the total of 18" as most informative for the biomarker signature. While test-set validation demonstrates 73% classification accuracy, the importance ranking itself lacks corroboration via permutation importance, SHAP, or other bias-robust methods.

title: Spatiotemporal patterns and drivers of soil organic carbon in black soil landscapes of Northeast China
evidence: The paper employs Random Forest with Liaw-Wiener Gini/MDI importance, explicitly reporting "relative importance" percentages (39.32% for PRE_SL, 17.83% for TMP_SL, 16.88% for NDVI_SL, 13.03% for NPP_SL) and concluding "Precipitation emerged as the most influential variable affecting SOC." These importance magnitudes and rankings drive practical policy recommendations: "governments should carefully consider...support sustainable land management...to promote long-term carbon sequestration." Corroboration is limited to Spearman correlation (r=0.330, -0.201, 0.107, 0.098) and test-set R²=0.78, neither of which validates importance magnitude/ranking against impurity bias. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance analysis is performed. With mixed-type features (continuous climate/vegetation, categorical land-use), likely precipitation skew, and resolution mismatches (1km, 250m, 30m resampled to 500m), impurity-bias mechanisms are plausible.

title: African soil properties and nutrients mapped at 30 m spatial resolution using two-scale ensemble machine learning
evidence: The paper ranks covariates by random forest impurity importance, stating "climatic images such as SM2RAIN monthly rainfall estimates and CHELSA bioclimatic images...are the most important covariates" and that "Sentinel-2 B11, B09 and B12, DTM vertical depth and Landsat SWIR1 are overall the most important." These rankings directly drive conclusions about which remote sensing products justify investment (25% of budget to Sentinel-2) and which geographic areas/nutrients need future sampling. The features are highly heterogeneous (mixed types, ~350/60 covariates, different resolutions and units) and target variables are explicitly log-normally distributed (right-skewed). No permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation corroborates the importance rankings.

title: Satellites can reveal global extent of forced labor in the world’s fishing fleet
evidence: The paper uses "unbiased corrected Gini index" for feature importance and states "The most important model features for identifying high-risk vessels were engine power, maximum distance from port, number of voyages per year, and average daily fishing hours." It then interprets this ranking substantively: "The importance of engine power, a proxy for vessel size, suggests that forced labor appears to cluster in similarly sized vessels" and "The importance of maximum distance from port and average daily fishing hours is consistent with expert opinion." No permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional, or held-out validation methods are used to corroborate the importance ranking.

title: Modeling Physiological Predictors of Running Velocity for Endurance Athletes
evidence: The paper states "The XGBoost machine learning approach was used to select variables with the highest prediction value" and concludes "The following variables showed the strongest impact in building the models: VO2, [La-]b, VE age, and BMI." The discussion interprets these rankings physiologically: "VO2 at RCP and maximal VO2 were the most important parameters responsible for the prediction." These importance rankings are supported only by internal cross-validation of predictive error (RMSE/MAE), without corroboration via permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance methods that would be robust to impurity-importance bias.

title: Trans-omics biomarker model improves prognostic prediction accuracy for early-stage lung adenocarcinoma
evidence: The paper uses ranger variable importance scoring to screen 12,806 DNA methylation probes down to 62 (discovery) and 38 (validation), and gene expression data down to 9 and 13 genes: "Ranger screened out 62 DNA methylation probes in the discovery phase according to variable importance score (VIS)." The core claim is "we identified 7 cancer-related genes and 12 DNA methylation probes as potentially associated with early-stage LUAD survival," which depends on this importance-based ranking. While the selected biomarkers are validated with Cox regression (FDR-corrected in discovery, p<0.05 in validation) and tested in independent cohorts, the paper provides no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance analysis to verify that the importance ranking itself is accurate.

title: Environmental predictors impact microbial-based postmortem interval (PMI) estimation models within human decomposition soils
evidence: The paper ranks features by MDI and claims "the most important model feature, as assessed by decrease in MSE, was the phylum Firmicutes" and "Of the environmental predictors assessed, electrical conductivity (EC) appeared to be most influential." These importance-based rankings drive biological interpretation: "Increases in Firmicutes and Clostridiales follow successional trends observed in internal microbial communities." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation corroborates these rankings on heterogeneous features (mixed types, 5000+ OTU-level taxa, skewed compositional data, scale mismatch with environmental parameters).

title: Digital mapping of soil texture in ecoforest polygons in Quebec, Canada
evidence: The paper uses ranger random forest and reports importance rankings as central findings: "The most important variables in our soil texture models are soil physical environment classes and soil parent material, followed by altitude and variables regarding climate" (Discussion, Variable importance section). The conclusion depends on this ranking: "Relative measures of variable importance in the models confirm that the characterization and delineation of soil characteristics by trained photo-interpreters are more relevant for soil texture mapping than climate and remote sensing covariates." No independent corroboration (permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional, or held-out validation) is provided to validate these importance magnitude/ranking claims.

title: Variations in Cycling Distances by Trip Purpose and Socio-Demographic Attributes: Implications for Spatial Scales to Assess Environmental Correlates of Cycling
evidence: Paper explicitly states: "We used an actual impurity reduction importance measure to estimate the relative importance of each explanatory variable." Main findings rest on ranking interpretation: "The trip purpose was identified as the most important factor...Gender was the second most important determinant (with a relative importance score of 21%)." This ranking directly informs policy: "An important implication of the findings is that purpose-specific buffer sizes need to be employed in order to capture environmental attributes related to cycling." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation methods appear in the paper to corroborate these rankings.

title: Classification of Molecular Subtypes of High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer by MALDI-Imaging
evidence: The paper selects features 'using Gini importance ranking' (Methods 4.6) and states that 'features associated with...ACTA2, HSPA8, H2A, and HSPD1 had the highest relevance' (Results 2.6). The discussion identifies specific proteins as predictive markers based on importance magnitude (e.g., H1.2 as 'highly discriminative'). However, only held-out test-set AUC validation is provided; the paper lacks permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance to corroborate the importance ranking.

title: Predicting the presence of depressive symptoms in the HIV-HCV co-infected population in Canada using supervised machine learning
evidence: The paper identifies and emphasizes impurity-importance rankings as primary findings: "Employment, HIV clinical stage, revenue source, body mass index (BMI), and education were the 5 most important predictors." In Discussion, it interprets these rankings to draw substantive conclusions: "The five most important predictors are related two main themes—i. SES (education, revenue source and employment) and ii. overall health status (HIV clinical stage and BMI)." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation is reported to corroborate the ranking.

title: Estimates of Lake Nitrogen, Phosphorus, and Chlorophyll‐a Concentrations to Characterize Harmful Algal Bloom Risk Across the United States
evidence: "Further variable selection was achieved by first running each RF model with all remaining predictors which allowed us to determine variable importance for each model predictor. We could then run a series of models starting with just the two most important predictors and iterating through all variables in order of importance." The paper explicitly structures conclusions around rankings: "Lake depth is the top predictor for TN by a large margin" and "TN and TP are by far the most important predictors of chlorophyll-a." These rankings support core findings presented as "the results of the RF models can be leveraged...to provide actionable information for management on predominant drivers of nutrient pollution." However, corroboration is limited to partial dependence plots showing directional relationships, with no permutation or SHAP validation of the rankings themselves.

title: An OMICs-based meta-analysis to support infection state stratification
evidence: The paper ranks genes by RF selection frequencies (Table 3), identifying 'LY6E, IFI27 and CD177 as important key genes' and biomarker panels based on which features were selected most often in backward elimination and genetic algorithm runs. These rankings are derived entirely from RF impurity importance via VarSelRF and GALGO. The paper validates predictive performance via cross-manufacturer held-out testing (Fig 5) and functional enrichment, confirming genes lie in immune-response pathways. However, no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance analysis is reported to validate whether the magnitude and ranking of individual gene importance is accurate. The impurity-importance bias could distort which genes are ranked highest without invalidating model accuracy.

title: In-Field Detection and Quantification of Septoria Tritici Blotch in Diverse Wheat Germplasm Using Spectral–Temporal Features
evidence: The authors acknowledge the bias risk, citing Strobl et al. (2007) on "Bias in random forest variable importance measures" and stating: "the presence of highly correlated predictors makes the interpretation of resulting variable importance measures challenging." Despite this, they use recursive feature elimination with random forest (which relies on impurity importance by default) and prominently feature the ranking: "The difference between M derived from the modified chlorophyll absorption ratio index (MCARI2) and the SIPI was consistently the most informative spectral-temporal feature" and "This feature was retained as the last in all 30 resamples by the random forest." Their conclusion directly acts on this ranking, recommending "Time-resolved measurements of the MCARI2 and the SIPI SVIs could allow to assess these traits at very high throughput." Validation (Pearson r=0.53, p<0.001 on independent data) confirms predictive power of the top feature but does not corroborate its ranking relative to other high-importance features; no permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance methods are employed.

title: Radiomics-based machine learning model for predicting secondary decompressive craniectomy in TBI patients after emergent craniotomy with bone flap replacement
evidence: The paper ranks features by Gini score and emphasizes that Maskoriginal_VolumeNum "had a Gini score significantly higher than all other features, underscoring its importance in the model." Feature selection is driven by ranking: "The top features, ranging from the top 5 to 20, were then used as inputs." However, SHAP values are mentioned only in supplementary material without discussion of whether they corroborate the Gini ranking, and no permutation/conditional importance is provided. In a 65-patient cohort with 112+ radiomic features mixed with categorical clinical variables, impurity importance will be biased toward high-cardinality features, yet the ranking is treated as ground truth for interpretation.

title: Kinome state is predictive of cell viability in pancreatic cancer tumor and cancer-associated fibroblast cell lines
evidence: The paper gathers variable importance data and identifies understudied kinases CSNK2A1/3, CAMKK2, and PIP4K2C as novel targets based on their high importance rankings: "CSNK2A1/3 was identified as highly ranking in the P0422-T1 tumor line" and "CAMKK2...is a top feature found only in the P0119-T1 CAF." The validation study (Fig. 5) confirms predictive accuracy on held-out compounds but does not corroborate the relative importance rankings themselves, only the binary viability predictions. No alternative importance methods (permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional) are applied to verify ranking stability.

title: Using Machine Learning to Predict Swine Movements within a Regional Program to Improve Control of Infectious Diseases in the US
evidence: The paper states: "The most important variables predicting movements were farm type (downstream combinations from finishers and farrowing farms to market sites), sharing the same owner, and distance (Figure 6)." This ranking is based entirely on impurity importance with no validation via permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance. The abstract and conclusion both emphasize which variables are "most important," yet the paper provides no corroboration of these importance magnitudes or rankings.

title: Machine learning based on metabolomics reveals potential targets and biomarkers for primary Sjogren’s syndrome
evidence: The paper uses RF importance to rank and select biomarkers: "on the basis of 21 metabolites, we screened the important variables in the models...five common variables were obtained by intersecting the important variables of three models...2-Hydroxypalmitic acid, L-carnitine and cyclic AMP were found to be potential targets and specific biomarkers for pSS." The main corroboration is SLE cohort validation for specificity, but this does not independently validate the importance ranking itself without permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional, or held-out methods. The biomarker discovery is entirely dependent on RF/XGBoost importance magnitude and ranking.

title: Simulation to investigate site-based monitoring of pest insect species for trade
evidence: The authors report "Lure attractiveness, release size, and trap density were the most important variables, and site shape, mean step size, and site size the least important" from the random forest MSE importance plot. They explicitly state "Lure attractiveness and trapping density had the greatest effect on pest detection" and conclude "These results were robust to the size and shape of the site" — a robustness claim grounded entirely in those variables ranking least important. No comparative validation against other methods (permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance) is presented; the GLM results mentioned in Supplementary Material are not compared to RF importance magnitudes or rankings.

title: Mast cell‐based molecular subtypes and signature associated with clinical outcome in early‐stage lung adenocarcinoma
evidence: Section 3.6 reports: "The results revealed that TP53 and CSMD3 were the most important gene mutations associated with the mast cell-related risk score" (Methods 2.8 uses ranger/randomforest packages without p-value testing). Figure 6 caption states "TP53 was the most important mutation according to the importance of ranking." The Discussion concludes "we further identified TP53 as the most critical mutation associated with the mast cell-related signature," making mechanistic claims about TP53-c-MYC-mast cell interactions based solely on impurity ranking without permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or held-out validation corroboration.

title: Impact of morphological traits and irrigation levels on fresh herbage yield of sorghum x sudangrass hybrid: Modelling data mining techniques
evidence: The paper reports RF importance using "Node Purity" (Gini impurity) in Table 6 and concludes: "The factors that determine the fresh herbage yield in SSG hybrid are ranked in order of significance as follows...and NDF, PH, and RFV for the RF algorithm." These importance rankings are listed as key findings. The paper provides no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance corroboration; instead it argues agreement across ANN and MARS (both impurity-based) validates the ranking. With high multicollinearity documented (VIF values up to 2748), the impurity importance magnitudes are unreliable.

title: Fish grabbing: Weak governance and productive waters are targets for distant water fishing
evidence: The authors explicitly interpret importance magnitude and ranking: "Governance effectiveness is the most important governance indicator in fixed gear, longliner and tuna purse seiner DWF, for the latter two it is the most informative across all variables" and "Tuna purse seiners are the only gear type in which governance indicators dominantly exceed biophysical variables in their Variable Importance scores." They use this ranking to argue that governance performance is unexpectedly important relative to traditional fisheries productivity theory. Corroboration is limited to ALE plots showing directional effects, with no permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation to confirm the ranking. Features combine six heterogeneous governance ordinals with continuous biophysical variables at different depths, all z-score standardized but inherently incommensurate.

title: Machine learning prediction of methionine and tryptophan photooxidation susceptibility
evidence: The paper states: "For random forest models, the importance was determined by the mean decrease in out-of-bag accuracy when that parameter was excluded from the model" and interprets rankings extensively: "Of note, both distance to the nearest Phe (Fd) and solvent exposure (SASA, PSA, SC_SASA, or PSSA) appear among the top features used by all models" and "In all predictive models of Met oxidation presented herein, Wd (distance to the nearest Trp) and Fd (distance to the nearest Phe) are among the most important parameters." No permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance methods are used to corroborate these rankings. Features show heterogeneous scales (distances, percentages, pH, concentrations) and the outcome is sparse (86/235 Met oxidized, 67/342 Trp oxidized).

title: What’s hot and what’s not: Making sense of biodiversity ‘hotspots’
evidence: The paper states: "The relative importance of the covariates in each Random Forest model was based on node impurity, a standard approach based on the reductions of the variance of the response variable each time that a covariate is used for a split." Results lean on ranking: "Regional factors relating to habitat heterogeneity, including variation in shear stress, depth and substrate...were important predictors of diversity...This suggests that regional- to ecosystem-scale processes chiefly determined assemblage structure...with small-scale habitat characteristics, such as substrate playing an important but subordinate role" and "Chlorophyll, phosphate and differences in dissolved oxygen concentrations ranked the most important gradients associated with anthropogenic pressure." Partial dependence plots are the only corroboration offered, but the authors caution they "may not be realistic and lead to misleading results in the case of strongly correlated variables." The ranking of variables (e.g., which factors are primary vs. subordinate drivers) directly supports their core conservation recommendation that regional-scale processes rather than local pressures should drive management priorities.

title: Toxicity Assessment of Mixed Exposure of Nine Perfluoroalkyl Substances at Concentrations Relevant to Daily Intake
evidence: The paper uses scikit-learn random forest with default impurity-based importance (not specified but standard Gini/MDI for classification). It ranks features explicitly: "5-aminovaleric acid as the most important compound" and "5-(Carbamoylamino)pentanoic acid, which had the second highest contribution in the random forest model." The authors interpret these rankings biologically: "The substance that contributed the most to the classification, 5-aminovaleric acid, is known to act as a weak GABA receptor agonist...may be associated with increased gene expression of Chrna4." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation was performed to corroborate the feature rankings.

title: Groundwater and Remotely Sensed Phenology Reveal Vulnerability of Riparian Trees to Drought
evidence: The paper states: "we used the 'vivid' package (Inglis et al. 2023) to estimate variable importance and its interaction effects for each predictor" and concludes: "photoperiod dominated at the perennial site, while DTW was the controlling factor at intermittent sites" (Section 3.3). Figure 5 visualizes importance via node size, and the text emphasizes rank: "photoperiod having the highest importance" at perennial sites versus "DTW...dominated" at intermittent sites. No permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional, or held-out validation of these importance rankings is provided; only a sensitivity analysis of model performance confirms precipitation lags offer "no significant improvement."

title: Closing the implementation-evidence gap using data science: a transferable workflow applied to medical cost analysis
evidence: The paper states: "RF regression with 50 trees and 5-fold cross-validation identified income, activity limitation, age, and chronic conditions as key predictors of medical expenditure" and "Across all specifications, chronic conditions consistently emerged as the most important variable of medical expenditure, confirming the stability, robustness, and reliability of the findings." The main conclusion is: "Statistical and machine learning analyses of the MEPS consistently identify chronic disease burden as the strongest predictor of total medical expenditure." This ranking-based interpretation relies entirely on impurity-importance measures from RF/GBR without permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional, or held-out importance validation.

title: Whole blood RNA sequencing identifies transcriptional differences between primary sclerosing cholangitis and ulcerative colitis
evidence: The paper explicitly uses "average Gini impurity increase" importance and ranks genes by it: "Not only known marker genes, such as S100A12 and S100A9, but also genes encoding ribosomal components and mitochondrial genes (examples including COX6B1, MT-RNR2, RPS9) were among the top 10 most important genes (Table S19)." For pathway enrichment, "we used the top 50% cumulated importance genes as input and weighted those by feature importance." Critically, validation is limited to predictive performance on held-out cohorts; there is no permutation importance, SHAP, or conditional importance analysis to validate the feature ranking itself. The conclusions about which genes distinguish UC from PSC rely substantially on this unvalidated importance ranking.

title: Analysis of Performance and Failure Modes of the IROC Proton Liver Phantom
evidence: The paper reports: "The highest ranking variables of importance for classification and all regression variables were irradiation year and PTV1 and PTV2 extent." However, univariate analysis contradicts this parity: extent predicts pass rate with R^2=0.71-0.85 (p=0.008-0.003), while year shows R^2=0.30 (p=0.20, non-significant). The ranking that year equals extent in importance is not corroborated by univariate regression. No permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance is provided to defend the ranked distinction between variables, leaving the ranking vulnerable to impurity bias magnification.

title: Role of intratumoral and peritumoral CT radiomics for the prediction of EGFR gene mutation in primary lung cancer
evidence: The paper explicitly uses "mean decrease in the Gini index" to measure feature importance and ranks features by magnitude: "The top 10 most important features were eight radiomic features, Brinkman index, and sex... Among the radiomic features, the intratumoral 90th percentile was the most important, followed by peritumoral large area high gray level emphasis (LAHGLE)." The Discussion interprets these importance rankings to support biological claims, stating "the most important peritumoral feature for predicting EGFR mutations was LAHGLE, with high values correlating with mutations. This radiomic feature measures the extent of a large area with a high CT value and can be elevated when high-density structures, such as large pulmonary vessels, are present around tumors." No alternative importance measures (permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional, or held-out validation) are provided to corroborate the ranking or magnitudes.

title: Predictors of adjustment to life after service among Canadian military veterans
evidence: The paper uses Gini impurity importance via ranger: "Node impurity was measured by the Gini index embedded within the R package ranger to determine variable importance." It interprets magnitude and ranking without corroboration: "The most important predictor of adjustment to civilian life was life satisfaction" and "the top ten most important predictors...in order of importance, were life satisfaction, mastery, mental health, age, BMI, social support, satisfaction with one's main activity, general health, income, and financial satisfaction." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation is used; multinomial logistic regression tests class differences on selected predictors but does not validate the importance rankings themselves.

title: IgG Antibody Responses to Epstein-Barr Virus in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: Their Effective Potential for Disease Diagnosis and Pathological Antigenic Mimicry
evidence: The paper ranks 3054 EBV peptide antibodies using mean decrease in Gini index and selects features by adding top-ranked antibodies until reaching 85% accuracy, yielding a 26-antibody diagnostic classifier. It interprets magnitude ("overall average antibody importance was around 0.018" vs. highest at 0.36) and ranking explicitly, stating "antibodies against this candidate antigen were not among the top most important antibodies for disease prediction," using this ranking interpretation to downplay molecular mimicry's pathogenic role. Corroboration is limited to held-out test accuracy (90%), without permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or alternative feature selection methods to verify whether MDI ranking identifies optimal biomarkers or whether magnitude/ranking would persist under different importance measures.

title: Characterizing Families of Spectral Similarity Scores and Their Use Cases for Gas Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry Small Molecule Identification
evidence: Section 2.4 explicitly states: "The relative importance of each variable in the random forest model was determined using the Gini Index." The results section ranks variables by importance: "Among all variables considered, the computed t-statistics of metrics is definitively the most important in dictating cluster group membership. Since, in this context, the t-statistic measures how different the true positive and non-true-positive distributions are from one another for each metric, the high importance of the t-statistic indicates that the four empirical cluster groups are largely determined by the discriminatory ability of each metric." No permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or other corroboration is provided for these magnitude and ranking conclusions, which form the paper's core interpretation of feature importance.

title: Modelling microclimatic variability in Andean forests of northern Patagonia
evidence: The paper explicitly interprets importance magnitude and ranking to identify "drivers of microclimatic variability": local macroclimatic data "exerted the most substantial influence, with its influence ranging from 50 to 80%"; TMIN at station is ranked #1 with 68.3% importance for soil temperature prediction; solar radiation, FAPAR, NDMI emerge as "key predictors" based on relative importance percentages. No alternative importance methods (permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional) are used to corroborate these rankings.

title: A multi-step machine learning approach to assess the impact of COVID-19 lockdown on NO2 attributable deaths in Milan and Rome, Italy
evidence: The paper reports LURF models built with ranger (which uses impurity-based importance by default) and identifies "most important predictors" as "traffic load for light vehicles and heavy and commercial vehicles and roads length" at specific buffer scales. This ranking is presented without permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out importance validation.
